extra_content: • The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) of Parliament thoroughly examines these reports.

title: Article 152
bare_text: Definition.
plain_english: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires, the expression 'State' does not include the State of Jammu and Kashmir.
example: Historically excluded J&K from the standard state rules due to Article 370.
extra_content: • Following the 2019 reorganization of J&K, the practical application of this definition has fundamentally changed.

title: Article 153
bare_text: Governors of States.
plain_english: There shall be a Governor for each State. However, the same person can be appointed as the Governor of two or more States.
example: The Governor of Maharashtra is often also given additional charge of Goa.
extra_content: • The 7th Amendment (1956) facilitated the appointment of the same person as Governor for multiple states.

title: Article 154
bare_text: Executive power of State.
plain_english: Just like the President is the executive head of the Union (Art 53), the Governor is the executive head of the State. All state executive actions are taken in their name.
example: When the State Government of Kerala signs an order, it is signed 'By order of the Governor'.
extra_content: • The Governor acts on the advice of the Chief Minister and the state cabinet.

title: Article 155
bare_text: Appointment of Governor.
plain_english: The Governor is NOT elected by the people. They are directly appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal.
example: Because they are appointed by the President (on the advice of the PM), the Governor acts as a vital link (and sometimes a point of friction) between the Centre and the State.
extra_content: • India rejected the American system of electing state governors to maintain tighter central control.

title: Article 156
bare_text: Term of office of Governor.
plain_english: The Governor holds office 'during the pleasure of the President'. Usually, the term is 5 years, but the President can dismiss them at any time without giving a reason.
example: When a new political party comes to power at the Centre, they often instantly fire and replace the Governors in all the states.
extra_content: • The Supreme Court has ruled that this dismissal cannot be purely arbitrary, but it remains a highly politicized process.

title: Article 157
bare_text: Qualifications for appointment as Governor.
plain_english: To be appointed Governor, a person must be a citizen of India and have completed the age of 35 years.
example: There are no educational or specific professional requirements in the Constitution to become a Governor.
extra_content: • By convention, a Governor is usually an outsider from another state to maintain neutrality.

title: Article 158
bare_text: Conditions of Governor's office.
plain_english: The Governor cannot be a member of Parliament or the State Legislature, and cannot hold any other 'office of profit'.
example: If an MP is appointed Governor, their Parliament seat automatically becomes vacant the moment they take office.
extra_content: • They are provided a rent-free official residence (Raj Bhavan).

title: Article 159
bare_text: Oath or affirmation by the Governor.
plain_english: Before taking office, the Governor takes an oath before the Chief Justice of the state's High Court.
example: The Governor swears to 'preserve, protect and defend the Constitution and the law'.
extra_content: • Different from the President, who takes the oath before the Chief Justice of India.

title: Article 160
bare_text: Discharge of the functions of the Governor in certain contingencies.
plain_english: The President can make special arrangements for discharging the functions of the Governor in any unexpected situation not covered by this chapter.
example: If a Governor suddenly dies and there is no Deputy Governor in India, the President can ask the Chief Justice of the High Court to temporarily take charge.
extra_content: • Provides an emergency backup plan for the state executive.

title: Article 161
bare_text: Power of Governor to grant pardons, etc., and to suspend, remit or commute sentences in certain cases.
plain_english: The Governor can pardon or reduce the sentence of any person convicted of a crime against a State Law.
example: A Governor can remit (reduce) the life sentence of a prisoner in a state jail for good behavior.
extra_content: • **Crucial Difference from President:** The Governor CANNOT pardon a death sentence (only the President can under Art 72), and cannot pardon Court Martial sentences.

title: Article 162
bare_text: Extent of executive power of State.
plain_english: The executive power of a State extends to the matters with respect to which the Legislature of the State has power to make laws.
example: Since 'Police' is a State List subject, the State Government has full executive control over the state police force.
extra_content: • In Concurrent List matters, the state's executive power is subject to the executive power of the Union.

title: Article 163
bare_text: Council of Ministers to aid and advise Governor.
plain_english: The Governor must act on the advice of the Chief Minister and the State Cabinet, except in matters where the Constitution gives the Governor 'discretion'.
example: Unlike the President (Art 74), the Governor actually has some discretionary powers, such as recommending President's Rule under Article 356.
extra_content: • If a question arises whether a matter falls under the Governor's discretion, the Governor's decision is final.

title: Article 164
bare_text: Other provisions as to Ministers.
plain_english: The Chief Minister is appointed by the Governor. The Ministers hold office 'during the pleasure of the Governor', but are collectively responsible to the State Legislative Assembly.
example: If the state government loses a floor test in the Assembly, the entire Council of Ministers must resign.
extra_content: • In states like Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, MP, and Odisha, there must be a minister in charge of tribal welfare.

title: Article 165
bare_text: Advocate-General for the State.
plain_english: Just like the Attorney General at the Centre, every State has an Advocate General appointed by the Governor to give legal advice to the state government.
example: When the State of Punjab is sued in the High Court, the Advocate General represents the state.
extra_content: • Must be qualified to be a High Court judge.

title: Article 166
bare_text: Conduct of business of the Government of a State.
plain_english: All executive action of the state government is formally taken in the name of the Governor.
example: The Governor makes rules regarding how files and orders are passed between ministers.
extra_content: • Ministers do the actual work, but the official orders bear the Governor's authority.

title: Article 167
bare_text: Duties of Chief Minister as respects the furnishing of information to Governor, etc.
plain_english: The Chief Minister must communicate all cabinet decisions to the Governor and furnish any information regarding state administration that the Governor asks for.
example: The Governor can demand an update from the CM regarding the state's handling of a severe riot.
extra_content: • Identical to the Prime Minister's duty to the President under Article 78.

title: Article 168
bare_text: Constitution of Legislatures in States.
plain_english: Every state has a Legislature consisting of the Governor and either one House (Legislative Assembly) or two Houses (Legislative Assembly and Legislative Council).
example: States like UP and Bihar have two houses (bicameral), while states like Kerala and Punjab only have one (unicameral).
extra_content: • The Governor is an integral part of the state legislature, just like the President is part of Parliament.

title: Article 169
bare_text: Abolition or creation of Legislative Councils in States.
plain_english: Parliament can create or abolish a State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) if the State Legislative Assembly passes a special resolution asking them to do so.
example: Andhra Pradesh passed a resolution in their assembly to abolish their Legislative Council, but the final power to abolish it lies with the Parliament in Delhi.
extra_content: • Only 6 states currently have a Legislative Council (Bicameral legislature).

title: Article 170
bare_text: Composition of the Legislative Assemblies.
plain_english: A State Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha) must have a maximum of 500 members and a minimum of 60 members, directly elected by the public.
example: Uttar Pradesh has the largest assembly (403 members), while smaller states like Sikkim have exceptions to the 60-member minimum rule.
extra_content: • Seats are reserved for SCs and STs based on their population ratio.

title: Article 171
bare_text: Composition of the Legislative Councils.
plain_english: The total number of members in the Legislative Council cannot exceed 1/3rd of the members in the Assembly. They are elected indirectly by teachers, graduates, MLAs, and local bodies.
example: The Governor also nominates members (like artists or scientists) to the Council.
extra_content: • Acts as a revising chamber, similar to the Rajya Sabha, but with far less power.

title: Article 172
bare_text: Duration of State Legislatures.
plain_english: The Legislative Assembly normally lasts for 5 years, unless dissolved earlier. The Legislative Council is a permanent body, but 1/3rd of its members retire every 2 years.
example: During a National Emergency, Parliament can extend the life of the state assembly by 1 year at a time.
extra_content: • Mirrors the duration rules of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.

title: Article 173
bare_text: Qualification for membership of the State Legislature.
plain_english: To be an MLA, you must be an Indian citizen and at least 25 years old. For the Legislative Council (MLC), the minimum age is 30.
example: A 26-year-old can become the Chief Minister if they get elected to the Assembly and lead the majority party.
extra_content: • Must also make an oath before the Election Commission during nomination.

title: Article 174
bare_text: Sessions of the State Legislature, prorogation and dissolution.
plain_english: The Governor summons the legislature, prorogues (ends the session), and dissolves the Legislative Assembly.
example: There cannot be a gap of more than 6 months between two sessions of the assembly.
extra_content: • Dissolving the assembly triggers fresh elections.

title: Article 175
bare_text: Right of Governor to address and send messages to the House or Houses.
plain_english: The Governor can address the state legislature and can also send messages to them regarding a pending bill.
example: The Governor might urge the assembly to prioritize a specific public health bill.
extra_content: • The legislature is bound to consider the message as soon as convenient.

title: Article 176
bare_text: Special address by the Governor.
plain_english: At the start of the first session after every general election and the first session of every year, the Governor MUST address the assembly.
example: This address outlines the policies and programs of the state government (written by the Chief Minister's cabinet).
extra_content: • Similar to the President's Address in Parliament under Art 87.

title: Article 177
bare_text: Rights of Ministers and Advocate-General as respects the Houses.
plain_english: Every Minister and the Advocate-General has the right to speak and take part in the proceedings of the state legislature, but the Advocate-General cannot vote.
example: If the Chief Minister is only a member of the Assembly, they can still go to the Council and give a speech there.
extra_content: • Allows the government to defend its policies in either house.

title: Article 178
bare_text: The Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Legislative Assembly.
plain_english: The Legislative Assembly must choose two of its members to be the Speaker and Deputy Speaker.
example: The Speaker is the ultimate boss of the assembly proceedings.
extra_content: • Usually elected from the ruling party, but supposed to act neutrally.

title: Article 179
bare_text: Vacation and resignation of, and removal from, the offices of Speaker and Deputy Speaker.
plain_english: Rules for how the Speaker or Deputy Speaker can resign (by writing to each other) or be removed by a majority resolution.
example: A 14-day notice is required before bringing a motion to remove the Speaker.
extra_content: • Almost identical to Article 94 for the Lok Sabha.

title: Article 180
bare_text: Power of the Deputy Speaker or other person to perform the duties of the office of, or to act as, Speaker.
plain_english: If the Speaker is absent or the post is vacant, the Deputy Speaker takes charge.
example: Ensures the Assembly always has a presiding officer to run the daily business.
extra_content: • If both are absent, the Governor appoints an acting Speaker.

title: Article 181
bare_text: The Speaker or the Deputy Speaker not to preside while a resolution for his removal from office is under consideration.
plain_english: The Speaker cannot preside over the Assembly if their own removal is being debated.
example: They can still speak and vote during the debate, but not act as the judge.
extra_content: • Ensures a fair proceeding against the presiding officer.

title: Article 182
bare_text: The Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Legislative Council.
plain_english: The Legislative Council chooses two of its members to be the Chairman and Deputy Chairman.
example: These officers run the upper house of the state, similar to the Speaker in the assembly.
extra_content: • Unlike the Rajya Sabha (where the VP is the ex-officio Chairman), the Council elects its own Chairman.

title: Article 183
bare_text: Vacation and resignation of, and removal from, the offices of Chairman and Deputy Chairman.
plain_english: Rules for the resignation or removal of the presiding officers of the Legislative Council.
example: They resign by addressing the resignation letter to each other.
extra_content: • Removal requires a majority of all the then members of the Council.

title: Article 184
bare_text: Power of the Deputy Chairman or other person to perform the duties of the office of, or to act as, Chairman.
plain_english: If the Chairman is absent, the Deputy Chairman runs the Legislative Council.
example: Standard succession rule for presiding officers.
extra_content: • If both are absent, the Governor appoints a temporary acting Chairman.

title: Article 185
bare_text: The Chairman or the Deputy Chairman not to preside while a resolution for his removal from office is under consideration.
plain_english: The Chairman cannot preside if their own removal is on the agenda.
example: Mirrors the rules for the Speaker of the Assembly.
extra_content: • Prevents conflict of interest.

title: Article 186
bare_text: Salaries and allowances of the Speaker and Deputy Speaker and the Chairman and Deputy Chairman.
plain_english: The state legislature passes a law to fix the salaries of its presiding officers.
example: These salaries are charged on the Consolidated Fund of the State.
extra_content: • Ensures financial independence for the presiding officers.

title: Article 187
bare_text: Secretariat of State Legislature.
plain_english: The state legislature has its own independent secretarial staff, separate from the state government bureaucracy.
example: Clerks and officers who manage the files, bills, and proceedings of the Vidhan Sabha.
extra_content: • Ensures the independence of the legislative branch from the executive branch.

title: Article 188
bare_text: Oath or affirmation by members.
plain_english: Every MLA and MLC must take an oath before the Governor before taking their seat.
example: They swear allegiance to the Constitution of India.
extra_content: • If they don't take the oath, they cannot vote or participate.

title: Article 189
bare_text: Voting in Houses, power of Houses to act notwithstanding vacancies and quorum.
plain_english: Decisions are made by majority vote. Quorum (minimum attendance) is 10 members or 1/10th of the total members, whichever is greater.
example: If only 8 MLAs show up to a 60-member assembly, the Speaker must suspend the meeting due to lack of quorum.
extra_content: • The Speaker only votes to break a tie.

title: Article 190
bare_text: Vacation of seats.
plain_english: An MLA loses their seat if they are absent without permission for 60 days, or if they resign, or get disqualified.
example: If an MLA sends a resignation letter to the Speaker, and the Speaker accepts it, the seat is instantly vacant.
extra_content: • You cannot be a member of both houses of the state legislature at the same time.

title: Article 191
bare_text: Disqualifications for membership.
plain_english: An MLA is disqualified for holding an 'office of profit', being of unsound mind, an undischarged insolvent, or losing citizenship.
example: Also, the Anti-Defection Law (10th Schedule) applies here if an MLA changes their political party after winning.
extra_content: • Mirrors Article 102 for Members of Parliament.

title: Article 192
bare_text: Decision on questions as to disqualifications of members.
plain_english: If a dispute arises about disqualification, the Governor decides the matter.
example: If an MLA is accused of holding a secret government contract (office of profit), the Governor gives the final verdict.
extra_content: • The Governor MUST act strictly on the advice of the Election Commission.

title: Article 193
bare_text: Penalty for sitting and voting before making oath under article 188 or when not qualified or when disqualified.
plain_english: A fine of Rs. 500 per day for anyone who sits or votes in the assembly while disqualified or before taking the oath.
example: A deterrent against illegal participation in lawmaking.
extra_content: • Recoverable as a debt to the State.

title: Article 194
bare_text: Powers, privileges, etc., of the Houses of Legislatures and of the members and committees thereof.
plain_english: Grants 'Parliamentary Privileges' to the state legislature. MLAs have freedom of speech in the assembly and cannot be sued in court for anything they say or any vote they cast there.
example: An MLA cannot be sued for defamation for a speech made on the floor of the Assembly.
extra_content: • Crucial for allowing lawmakers to debate fearlessly.

title: Article 195
bare_text: Salaries and allowances of members.
plain_english: MLAs and MLCs receive salaries as decided by the state legislature by law.
example: The Assembly passes a bill to determine how much the MLAs get paid.
extra_content: • Varies significantly from state to state.

title: Article 196
bare_text: Provisions as to introduction and passing of Bills.
plain_english: A regular bill can originate in either house. It must be passed by the houses before going to the Governor.
example: Unlike Parliament, there is no provision for a 'Joint Sitting' in the state legislature if the two houses disagree.
extra_content: • A pending bill does not lapse just because the house is prorogued.

title: Article 197
bare_text: Restriction on powers of Legislative Council as to Bills other than Money Bills.
plain_english: Unlike the Rajya Sabha, the State Legislative Council cannot block an ordinary bill. It can only delay it for a maximum of 4 months (3 months first time + 1 month second time).
example: If the UP Assembly passes a bill and the Council rejects it, the Assembly can just pass it a second time and it bypasses the Council entirely.
extra_content: • Shows the utter supremacy of the Legislative Assembly over the Council.

title: Article 198
bare_text: Special procedure in respect of Money Bills.
plain_english: A Money Bill can ONLY be introduced in the Legislative Assembly, not the Council. The Council can only delay it by a maximum of 14 days.
example: If the state government wants to raise a new tax, the bill must start in the Vidhan Sabha. If the Vidhan Parishad tries to block it, it automatically passes after 14 days anyway.
extra_content: • Ensures that the directly elected house has absolute control over the state's finances.

title: Article 199
bare_text: Definition of 'Money Bills'.
plain_english: Defines what makes a bill a 'Money Bill' in a state (taxation, borrowing money, Consolidated Fund of the State).
example: A bill regulating local municipal taxes is NOT a state Money Bill.
extra_content: • The Speaker of the Legislative Assembly has the final, unquestionable say on whether a bill is a Money Bill.

title: Article 200
bare_text: Assent to Bills.
plain_english: When a bill is passed by the state legislature, it goes to the Governor. The Governor can sign it, withhold assent, return it, or RESERVE it for the President's consideration.
example: If a state passes a bill that threatens the powers of the High Court, the Governor MUST reserve it for the President to review.
extra_content: • This is a crucial tool of central control over state legislation.

title: Article 201
bare_text: Bills reserved for consideration.
plain_english: When a bill is reserved by the Governor, the President can either give assent or direct the Governor to return it to the state legislature.
example: The President has absolute veto power over state bills reserved for him; there is no time limit for him to decide.
extra_content: • The state legislature cannot override the President's veto.

title: Article 202
bare_text: Annual financial statement.
plain_english: The Governor must present the State Budget ('Annual Financial Statement') to the legislature every financial year, showing estimated receipts and expenditures.
example: The State Finance Minister reads the budget speech, detailing how much the state will spend on roads, schools, and hospitals.
extra_content: • Splits expenses into 'charged' (non-votable) and 'made from' (votable) the Consolidated Fund.

title: Article 203
bare_text: Procedure in Legislature with respect to estimates.
plain_english: Votable expenses are presented as 'Demands for Grants'. The Assembly can approve, refuse, or reduce a grant, but cannot increase it.
example: The Assembly can vote to cut the education budget by Rs. 100 as a symbolic protest (cut motion), but they can't vote to increase it.
extra_content: • No demand for a grant can be made except on the recommendation of the Governor.

title: Article 204
bare_text: Appropriation Bills.
plain_english: After the budget is voted on, an Appropriation Bill must be passed to legally authorize the government to actually withdraw the money from the state's bank account.
example: Even if the assembly approves a road project, the treasury won't release the funds until this bill becomes law.
extra_content: • No amendment can be proposed to this bill that would alter the destination of a grant already agreed to.

title: Article 205
bare_text: Supplementary, additional or excess grants.
plain_english: If the state runs out of money for a project, or needs to fund a new emergency, the Governor asks for supplementary grants.
example: If massive floods destroy infrastructure, the state will ask for supplementary funds to rebuild.
extra_content: • Follows the same strict procedure as the main budget.

title: Article 206
bare_text: Votes on account, votes of credit and exceptional grants.
plain_english: Allows the Assembly to give the government a 'Vote on Account' (advance money) to keep running for a few months while the full budget is being debated.
example: Crucial during an election year when the new government hasn't had time to draft a full budget.
extra_content: • Mirrors Article 116 at the Union level.

title: Article 207
bare_text: Special provisions as to financial Bills.
plain_english: Financial Bills (which include some financial matters but aren't pure Money Bills) must also be introduced in the Assembly on the Governor's recommendation.
example: A bill establishing a new state university that also involves some government funding.
extra_content: • The Legislative Council has more power over Financial Bills than Money Bills.

title: Article 208
bare_text: Rules of procedure.
plain_english: The state legislature can make rules to regulate its own procedure and conduct of business.
example: Setting the rules for how Question Hour is conducted in the Vidhan Sabha.
extra_content: • The Governor makes rules for joint communications between the two houses.

title: Article 209
bare_text: Regulation by law of procedure in the Legislature of the State in relation to financial business.
plain_english: The state can pass a strict law regulating how financial business is conducted to ensure the budget is passed on time.
example: Prevents opposition MLAs from filibustering (talking endlessly) to delay the state budget.
extra_content: • Overrides the general rules of procedure if there is a conflict.

title: Article 210
bare_text: Language to be used in the Legislature.
plain_english: Business in the state legislature is transacted in the official language of the state, Hindi, or English. The Speaker can allow an MLA to speak in their mother tongue.
example: In Tamil Nadu, the business is primarily in Tamil, but an MLA could potentially speak in Telugu with permission.
extra_content: • English was originally meant to be phased out after 15 years, unless the state legislature passed a law keeping it.

title: Article 211
bare_text: Restriction on discussion in the Legislature.
plain_english: The state legislature is absolutely forbidden from discussing the conduct of any High Court or Supreme Court Judge.
example: An MLA cannot use the assembly floor to attack a judge who recently gave a verdict against their political party.
extra_content: • Protects the judiciary from political pressure at the state level.

title: Article 212
bare_text: Courts not to inquire into proceedings of the Legislature.
plain_english: Courts cannot invalidate any proceedings in the state legislature on the grounds of 'procedural irregularity'.
example: If the Speaker forgets to ring the quorum bell, a court won't cancel the bill that was passed that day.
extra_content: • Protects the sovereignty of the legislature in its own domain.

title: Article 213
bare_text: Power of Governor to promulgate Ordinances during recess of Legislature.
plain_english: The Governor can issue Ordinances (temporary laws) when the state legislature is not in session, just like the President does under Article 123.
example: A state government urgently passing a law to ban an addictive substance via an ordinance before the assembly meets.
extra_content:
• Must be approved by the state legislature within 6 weeks of reassembly.
  • For certain subjects, the Governor needs prior instructions from the President.

title: Article 214
bare_text: High Courts for States.
plain_english: There shall be a High Court for each State.
example: The Allahabad High Court is the highest judicial authority in Uttar Pradesh.
extra_content: • Parliament can establish a common High Court for two or more states (Article 231), like the Punjab and Haryana High Court.

title: Article 215
bare_text: High Courts to be courts of record.
plain_english: Like the Supreme Court, every High Court is a 'Court of Record' and has the power to punish for contempt of itself.
example: The judgments of the Bombay High Court are binding on all lower district courts in Maharashtra.
extra_content: • Ensures judicial discipline within the state.

title: Article 216
bare_text: Constitution of High Courts.
plain_english: Every High Court shall consist of a Chief Justice and such other Judges as the President may from time to time deem it necessary to appoint.
example: The President decides how many judges the Allahabad High Court needs compared to the Sikkim High Court.
extra_content: • There is no fixed maximum number of judges in the Constitution for a High Court.

title: Article 217
bare_text: Appointment and conditions of the office of a Judge of a High Court.
plain_english: High Court judges are appointed by the President (not the Governor) after consulting the CJI, the State Governor, and the Chief Justice of the High Court.
example: A High Court judge retires at the age of 62 (unlike SC judges who retire at 65).
extra_content: • They can only be removed through the same difficult impeachment process as an SC judge.

title: Article 218
bare_text: Application of certain provisions relating to Supreme Court to High Courts.
plain_english: The complex process for removing (impeaching) a Supreme Court judge (Article 124(4) and (5)) applies exactly the same way to High Court judges.
example: A High Court judge can only be removed by an order of the President after a massive majority vote in Parliament.
extra_content: • Ensures high security of tenure for High Court judges.

title: Article 219
bare_text: Oath or affirmation by Judges of High Courts.
plain_english: Before taking office, a High Court Judge must take an oath before the Governor of the state.
example: They swear to bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India.
extra_content: • Different from Supreme Court judges, who take the oath before the President.

title: Article 220
bare_text: Restriction on practice after being a permanent Judge.
plain_english: A person who has been a permanent Judge of a High Court cannot practice law in that same High Court or any court below it.
example: A retired judge of the Bombay High Court can only practice in the Supreme Court or a different High Court (like Delhi).
extra_content: • Prevents them from having undue influence over their former junior colleagues.

title: Article 221
bare_text: Salaries, etc., of Judges.
plain_english: High Court judges receive salaries determined by Parliament, charged on the Consolidated Fund of the State.
example: Their salary cannot be reduced to their disadvantage after appointment.
extra_content: • Interestingly, while the State pays their salary, their pension is paid by the Union (Centre).

title: Article 222
bare_text: Transfer of a Judge from one High Court to another.
plain_english: The President can transfer a Judge from one High Court to any other High Court.
example: A judge can be transferred from the Madras High Court to the Patna High Court.
extra_content: • The President MUST consult the Chief Justice of India before doing this (Colledgium system).

title: Article 223
bare_text: Appointment of acting Chief Justice.
plain_english: If the office of the Chief Justice of a High Court is vacant, the President appoints another judge of that court to act as the Chief Justice.
example: Usually, the senior-most judge takes this role temporarily.
extra_content: • Ensures administrative continuity in the court.

title: Article 224
bare_text: Appointment of additional and acting Judges.
plain_english: If a High Court has too many pending cases, the President can appoint 'Additional Judges' for a temporary period (up to 2 years).
example: Used to tackle massive judicial backlogs.
extra_content: • They must retire at the age of 62, just like permanent judges.

title: Article 225
bare_text: Jurisdiction of existing High Courts.
plain_english: Preserved the existing jurisdiction and powers of the High Courts that existed before the Constitution was adopted.
example: Ensured that the historic Madras, Bombay, and Calcutta High Courts didn't lose their established powers.
extra_content: • A transitional provision ensuring legal continuity.

title: Article 226: Power of High Courts to issue certain writs
bare_text: Power of High Courts to issue certain writs.
plain_english: The High Court can issue writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, etc.) to enforce Fundamental Rights AND for 'any other purpose' (like ordinary legal rights).
example: If your passport is arbitrarily revoked, you can file a writ petition in the High Court under Article 226.
extra_content: • The writ jurisdiction of the High Court is actually WIDER than that of the Supreme Court (which can only issue writs for Fundamental Rights under Art 32).

title: Article 227
bare_text: Power of superintendence over all courts by the High Court.
plain_english: The High Court has administrative and judicial control over all subordinate courts and tribunals throughout its territorial jurisdiction.
example: The High Court can call for records from a lower District Court to ensure they are following the rules.
extra_content: • Excludes courts/tribunals constituted under any law relating to the Armed Forces.

title: Article 228
bare_text: Transfer of certain cases to High Court.
plain_english: If a High Court sees that a case pending in a lower district court involves a major question of Constitutional interpretation, it can pull the case up to itself.
example: If a district court is confused about whether a local law violates Fundamental Rights, the High Court will take over the case.
extra_content: • The High Court can either decide the whole case itself or just answer the constitutional question and send it back down.

title: Article 229
bare_text: Officers and servants and the expenses of High Courts.
plain_english: The Chief Justice of the High Court appoints the staff of the court. The administrative expenses are charged on the state's Consolidated Fund.
example: The state government cannot cut the High Court's budget or fire its clerks as political retaliation.
extra_content: • Ensures the administrative independence of the High Court from the state executive.

title: Article 230
bare_text: Extension of jurisdiction of High Courts to Union territories.
plain_english: Parliament can pass a law to extend the jurisdiction of a State's High Court to cover a Union Territory.
example: The Kerala High Court also covers the UT of Lakshadweep. The Calcutta High Court covers Andaman and Nicobar Islands.
extra_content: • A state legislature cannot do this; only Parliament can.

title: Article 231
bare_text: Establishment of a common High Court for two or more States.
plain_english: Parliament can establish one single High Court that serves two or more states.
example: The Punjab and Haryana High Court serves both states and the UT of Chandigarh.
extra_content: • Also, the Bombay High Court serves Maharashtra and Goa.

title: Article 232
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: Repealed by the Constitution (Seventh Amendment) Act, 1956.
example: Previously dealt with interpretations related to High Courts for States.
extra_content: • No longer active.

title: Article 233
bare_text: Appointment of district judges.
plain_english: District judges are appointed by the Governor in consultation with the High Court.
example: This is the highest judicial authority at the district level. To be appointed directly, a person must have been an advocate for at least 7 years.
extra_content: • Ensures that the executive cannot pack the lower courts without High Court approval.

title: Article 234
bare_text: Recruitment of persons other than district judges to the judicial service.
plain_english: Appointments to the lower state judicial service (civil judges, magistrates) are made by the Governor in accordance with rules made after consulting the State Public Service Commission and the High Court.
example: When a law graduate clears the State Judicial Services exam, they are appointed under this article.
extra_content: • Ensures the lower judiciary is recruited based on merit, not political favor.

title: Article 235
bare_text: Control over subordinate courts.
plain_english: The High Court has complete administrative and disciplinary control over all district and lower courts in the state.
example: The High Court decides the postings, promotions, and transfers of lower court judges, not the Chief Minister.
extra_content: • A massive safeguard ensuring the independence of the lower judiciary from the state executive.

title: Article 236
bare_text: Interpretation.
plain_english: Defines what a 'district judge' is (includes city civil court judge, additional district judge, etc.) and what 'judicial service' means.
example: Legal definitions for Part VI, Chapter VI.
extra_content: • Clarifies terminology used in the preceding articles.

title: Article 237
bare_text: Application of the provisions of this Chapter to certain class or classes of magistrates.
plain_english: The Governor can issue a public notification to apply the rules of this chapter (protecting judicial independence) to executive magistrates.
example: Used to separate the judiciary from the executive at the lowest levels of administration.
extra_content: • Aligns with the Directive Principle in Article 50 (separation of judiciary from executive).

title: Article 238
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: Repealed by the Constitution (Seventh Amendment) Act, 1956.
example: Previously contained the entirety of Part VII, dealing with the administration of Part B States (former Princely States).
extra_content: • Abolished when the distinction between Part A and Part B states was removed.

title: Article 239
bare_text: Administration of Union territories.
plain_english: Every Union Territory is administered by the President acting through an 'Administrator' appointed by him.
example: The Lieutenant Governor of Delhi or the Administrator of Lakshadweep acts on behalf of the President.
extra_content: • The President can also appoint the Governor of a neighboring state to act as the administrator of a UT.

title: Article 240
bare_text: Power of President to make regulations for certain Union territories.
plain_english: The President can make direct regulations for the peace, progress, and good government of UTs like Andaman & Nicobar, Lakshadweep, and Dadra & Nagar Haveli.
example: A regulation made under this article has the exact same force as an Act of Parliament.
extra_content: • Provides a simplified lawmaking process for smaller, directly governed territories.

title: Article 241
bare_text: High Courts for Union territories.
plain_english: Parliament can establish a dedicated High Court for a Union Territory, or declare any existing court there to be a High Court.
example: Under this article, Parliament established the Delhi High Court.
extra_content: • Delhi is the only UT with its own independent High Court.

title: Article 242
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: This article was repealed by the Constitution (Seventh Amendment) Act, 1956.
example: Previously dealt with the special state of Coorg, which was merged into Mysore (Karnataka).
extra_content: • No longer part of active law.

title: Article 243
bare_text: Definitions (Panchayats).
plain_english: Introduces the Panchayati Raj system. Defines 'Gram Sabha' as a body consisting of all persons registered in the electoral rolls at the village level.
example: The Gram Sabha is the foundation of local self-government, acting like a village parliament.
extra_content: • Added by the historic 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act (1992).

title: Article 243A
bare_text: Gram Sabha.
plain_english: A Gram Sabha may exercise such powers and perform such functions at the village level as the Legislature of a State may, by law, provide.
example: The Gram Sabha consists of all registered voters in the village and acts as the foundational general assembly of the Panchayat system.
extra_content: • It is the ultimate grassroots democratic body.

category: Part X: Scheduled and Tribal Areas
title: Article 244
bare_text: Administration of Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas.
plain_english: The provisions of the Fifth Schedule apply to the administration and control of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in any State OTHER than Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram (which are covered by the Sixth Schedule).
example: Provides special administrative structures (like Tribal Advisory Councils) to protect tribal lands and customs in states like Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh.
extra_content: • A crucial protection mechanism for indigenous populations against exploitation.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 245
bare_text: Extent of laws made by Parliament and by the Legislatures of States.
plain_english: Parliament can make laws for the whole or any part of India, and its laws can apply to Indian citizens anywhere in the world (extraterritorial operation). State legislatures can only make laws for their specific state.
example: If Parliament bans cybercrime, an Indian citizen hacking from London can still be prosecuted under that law. A law passed by the Gujarat assembly only applies within Gujarat.
extra_content: • Establishes the fundamental territorial limits of legislative power.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 246
bare_text: Subject-matter of laws made by Parliament and by the Legislatures of States.
plain_english: Divides law-making power into three lists (Seventh Schedule): Union List (only Parliament can make laws), State List (only States can make laws), and Concurrent List (both can make laws).
example: Defense is in the Union List. Police is in the State List. Education is in the Concurrent List.
extra_content: • The core article establishing the federal distribution of powers.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 247
bare_text: Power of Parliament to provide for the establishment of certain additional courts.
plain_english: Parliament can establish additional courts (besides the Supreme Court and High Courts) for the better administration of laws made by Parliament on Union List subjects.
example: Parliament could create specialized federal courts to deal exclusively with customs or copyright law.
extra_content: • Allows the creation of specialized central tribunals.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 248
bare_text: Residuary powers of legislation.
plain_english: If a completely new subject arises that is NOT mentioned in the Union, State, or Concurrent lists, ONLY the Parliament has the power to make laws on it.
example: When the internet was invented, laws regarding Information Technology (which wasn't in any list) were made exclusively by Parliament using its residuary power.
extra_content: • Unlike the US Constitution, where residuary powers belong to the states.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 249
bare_text: Power of Parliament to legislate with respect to a matter in the State List in the national interest.
plain_english: If the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution with a 2/3rds majority declaring it necessary in the 'national interest', Parliament can temporarily make laws on subjects in the State List.
example: If agricultural supply chains break down nationally, Rajya Sabha can authorize Parliament to pass a temporary nationwide agricultural law.
extra_content: • Demonstrates how the Indian Constitution can act like a unitary system during normal times.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 250
bare_text: Power of Parliament to legislate with respect to any matter in the State List if a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation.
plain_english: During a National Emergency, Parliament gets the absolute power to make laws on ANY subject, even those normally reserved exclusively for the States (State List).
example: If a war breaks out, Parliament can instantly pass laws controlling state police or agriculture.
extra_content: • These laws expire 6 months after the emergency ends. Transforms India into a unitary state.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 251
bare_text: Inconsistency between laws made by Parliament under articles 249 and 250 and laws made by the Legislatures of States.
plain_english: If Parliament makes a law on a state subject during an Emergency (Art 250) or Rajya Sabha resolution (Art 249), and a state law contradicts it, the Parliamentary law wins.
example: The state law isn't dead, but it goes to sleep (becomes inoperative) as long as the overriding Parliamentary law exists.
extra_content: • Ensures federal supremacy during exceptional circumstances.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 252
bare_text: Power of Parliament to legislate for two or more States by consent and adoption of such legislation by any other State.
plain_english: If two or more states pass a resolution asking Parliament to make a law on a State List subject for them, Parliament can do so.
example: The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 was passed by Parliament because multiple states asked for a uniform law.
extra_content: • Once passed, only Parliament can amend or repeal it, not the states.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 253
bare_text: Legislation for giving effect to international agreements.
plain_english: Parliament has absolute power to make any law for the whole country to implement international treaties or agreements, even if it touches a State List subject.
example: If India signs a UN climate treaty, Parliament can pass strict environmental laws that override state powers to ensure compliance.
extra_content: • Ensures that states cannot sabotage India's foreign policy commitments.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 254
bare_text: Inconsistency between laws made by Parliament and laws made by the Legislatures of States.
plain_english: If both Parliament and a State make a law on a Concurrent List subject and they contradict each other, the Central law prevails and the State law becomes void.
example: If the Centre says environmental clearance takes 30 days, and a State says 90 days, the Central law overrides the state law.
extra_content: • Exception: If the state law was reserved for and received the President's assent, it prevails in that specific state.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 255
bare_text: Requirements as to recommendations and previous sanctions to be regarded as matters of procedure only.
plain_english: If a law required the President's or Governor's prior recommendation to be introduced, but somehow passed without it and they assented to it later, the law is still perfectly valid.
example: A technical shield to prevent laws from being struck down over minor procedural hiccups.
extra_content: • Courts cannot invalidate the Act on this technicality.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 256
bare_text: Obligation of States and the Union.
plain_english: Every State must exercise its executive power to ensure compliance with the laws made by Parliament, and the Centre can give directions to the States to ensure this.
example: If Parliament passes a national highway law, the State police must ensure it is enforced in their state.
extra_content: • Failure to comply can lead to President's Rule under Article 365.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 257
bare_text: Control of the Union over States in certain cases.
plain_english: States must use their executive power so they don't impede the Union government. The Centre can give direct orders to states regarding national highways, waterways, and railways.
example: The Centre can order a state to deploy police to protect a vital railway line running through that state.
extra_content: • If the state disobeys these directions, President's Rule (Art 356) can be imposed via Art 365.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 258
bare_text: Power of the Union to confer powers, etc., on States in certain cases.
plain_english: The President can, with the state government's consent, entrust Union executive functions to state officers.
example: The Centre can ask state police officers to enforce a Central environmental law.
extra_content: • The Centre pays the state for the extra costs incurred. Promotes cooperative federalism.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 259
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: Repealed by the Constitution (Seventh Amendment) Act, 1956.
example: Dealt with Armed Forces in Part B states.
extra_content: • No longer part of active law.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 260
bare_text: Jurisdiction of the Union in relation to territories outside India.
plain_english: The Government of India can undertake executive, legislative, or judicial functions in a territory outside India by an agreement with that territory's government.
example: Before Sikkim officially became a state, India handled its defense and foreign affairs based on a treaty.
extra_content: • Subject to the Foreign Jurisdiction Act.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 261
bare_text: Public acts, records and judicial proceedings.
plain_english: Full faith and credit must be given throughout India to public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of the Union and of every State.
example: A marriage certificate issued in Kerala is fully legally valid in Punjab. A court judgment from Delhi can be enforced in Tamil Nadu.
extra_content: • Crucial for maintaining a unified legal system across the country.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 262
bare_text: Adjudication of disputes relating to waters of inter-State rivers or river valleys.
plain_english: Parliament can make laws to resolve disputes between states over river waters. Parliament can also completely exclude the Supreme Court from interfering in these disputes.
example: The setting up of the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu.
extra_content: • Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956 was passed under this article.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 263
bare_text: Provisions with respect to an inter-State Council.
plain_english: The President can establish an Inter-State Council to investigate disputes between states and discuss subjects of common interest.
example: Used to coordinate policies regarding health, education, and internal security across all states.
extra_content: • Set up permanently in 1990 based on the Sarkaria Commission recommendations.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 264
bare_text: Interpretation.
plain_english: In this Part (Finance), 'Finance Commission' means a Finance Commission constituted under article 280.
example: A simple definitional clause for the finance chapter.
extra_content: • Originally contained more, but was amended.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 265
bare_text: Taxes not to be imposed save by authority of law.
plain_english: No tax can be levied or collected by the government except by the authority of a law passed by the legislature.
example: A police officer or a minister cannot arbitrarily demand a 'road tax' from you unless the Legislative Assembly has officially passed a law creating that tax.
extra_content: • A foundational principle protecting citizens from arbitrary extortion by the executive.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 266
bare_text: Consolidated Funds and public accounts of India and of the States.
plain_english: Creates the 'Consolidated Fund of India' into which ALL revenues received, loans raised, and money recovered by the government must be deposited.
example: Every single rupee collected as Income Tax goes directly into the Consolidated Fund.
extra_content: • No money can be withdrawn from this fund without Parliamentary approval (Appropriation Bill).

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 267
bare_text: Contingency Fund.
plain_english: Creates an emergency fund placed at the disposal of the President to meet unforeseen, urgent expenditures without waiting for Parliamentary approval.
example: If a massive earthquake hits, the President can immediately release funds for rescue operations from the Contingency Fund.
extra_content: • The fund is later replenished from the Consolidated Fund after Parliament retroactively approves it.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 268
bare_text: Duties levied by the Union but collected and appropriated by the States.
plain_english: Certain taxes (like stamp duties on bills of exchange) are levied by the Central government, but the States physically collect the money and get to keep it all.
example: If you buy a massive insurance policy, the stamp duty is set by the Centre, but your state government pockets the cash.
extra_content: • This money does not go into the Centre's Consolidated Fund.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 269
bare_text: Taxes levied and collected by the Union but assigned to the States.
plain_english: Taxes on the sale or purchase of goods in the course of inter-state trade (CST) are levied and collected by the Centre, but the money is entirely handed over to the states.
example: If a factory in Gujarat sells goods to a shop in Maharashtra, the Centre collects the tax and gives it to the states based on Parliament's rules.
extra_content: • Heavily modified by the introduction of GST (Article 269A).

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 270
bare_text: Taxes levied and distributed between the Union and the States.
plain_english: All central taxes (like Income Tax, Corporate Tax, CGST) form a massive pool. This pool is then shared between the Centre and the States based on the Finance Commission's recommendations.
example: Currently, roughly 41% of the central tax pool is given back to the states.
extra_content: • Cesses and surcharges are NOT shared; the Centre keeps them entirely.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 271
bare_text: Surcharge on certain duties and taxes for purposes of the Union.
plain_english: Parliament can add a 'surcharge' (an extra tax on top of a tax) to any central tax, and the Centre gets to keep 100% of this surcharge money.
example: A 'Health and Education Cess' added on top of your income tax goes entirely to the Central Government; states don't get a cut.
extra_content: • A powerful tool for the Centre to raise exclusive revenue.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 272
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: Repealed by the Constitution (Eightieth Amendment) Act, 2000.
example: Previously dealt with taxes which are levied and collected by the Union and may be distributed between the Union and the States.
extra_content: • Replaced by the newer system in Art 270.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 273
bare_text: Grants in lieu of export duty on jute and jute products.
plain_english: Provided transitional grants to Assam, Bihar, Odisha, and West Bengal in lieu of their share of the jute export duty after independence.
example: This was a temporary financial band-aid for jute-producing states.
extra_content: • Expired 10 years after the commencement of the Constitution. Obsolete now.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 274
bare_text: Prior recommendation of President required to Bills affecting taxation in which States are interested.
plain_english: No bill that changes a tax which affects the States, or changes how money is shared with the States, can be introduced in Parliament without the President's prior recommendation.
example: Protects the states from sudden financial shocks caused by private members introducing tax-altering bills.
extra_content: • A safeguard for state finances.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 275
bare_text: Grants from the Union to certain States.
plain_english: Parliament can provide financial grants-in-aid to specific states that are in need of assistance, especially for tribal welfare.
example: The Centre giving extra funds to Assam or Odisha to boost development in scheduled tribal areas.
extra_content: • These are 'Statutory Grants' charged directly on the Consolidated Fund.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 276
bare_text: Taxes on professions, trades, callings and employments.
plain_english: State governments (or local municipalities) can levy a small tax on professions, trades, and employments. However, this tax cannot exceed Rs. 2,500 per year per person.
example: Many states deduct a small 'Professional Tax' from the salaries of corporate employees.
extra_content: • This is not considered an income tax (which only the Centre can levy).

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 277
bare_text: Savings.
plain_english: Any taxes or duties being lawfully levied by a state or municipality before the Constitution began (1950) could continue to be levied, even if those taxes were now in the Union List.
example: A grandfather clause to prevent sudden financial collapse of local bodies after independence.
extra_content: • Valid until Parliament passes a law to the contrary.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 278
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: Repealed by the Constitution (Seventh Amendment) Act, 1956.
example: Dealt with an agreement with Part B states.
extra_content: • No longer active.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 279
bare_text: Calculation of 'net proceeds', etc.
plain_english: The 'net proceeds' of a tax (the money left over after deducting the cost of collecting it) is calculated and certified by the Comptroller and Auditor-General of India (CAG).
example: If the Centre collects Rs 100 in tax, but it costs Rs 5 to run the tax department, the 'net proceeds' shared with states is Rs 95. The CAG certifies this math.
extra_content: • The CAG's certificate is final.

title: Article 280: Finance Commission
bare_text: Finance Commission.
plain_english: The President must constitute a Finance Commission every 5 years to recommend how tax revenues should be distributed between the Centre and the States.
example: The 15th Finance Commission recommended that 41% of central taxes be given to the states.
extra_content: • Acts as the balancing wheel of fiscal federalism in India.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 281
bare_text: Recommendations of the Finance Commission.
plain_english: The President must lay every recommendation made by the Finance Commission, along with an explanatory memorandum on the action taken, before both Houses of Parliament.
example: Forces the government to publicly explain whether they accepted or rejected the Finance Commission's formula for sharing money with the states.
extra_content: • Ensures transparency in federal finance.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 282
bare_text: Expenditure defrayable by the Union or a State out of its revenues.
plain_english: Both the Centre and the States can make 'grants' for any public purpose, even if that purpose is not strictly within their legislative power.
example: The Central government can give a massive financial grant to states for a specific health program, even though 'Public Health' is technically a State List subject.
extra_content: • The foundation for massive Centrally Sponsored Schemes (like MGNREGA).

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 283
bare_text: Custody, etc., of Consolidated Funds, Contingency Funds and moneys credited to the public accounts.
plain_english: Parliament (and State Legislatures) make laws regulating how government money is paid into and withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund, Contingency Fund, and Public Account.
example: Ensures there are strict legal rules and accounting standards for handling the nation's treasury.
extra_content: • Prevents arbitrary spending by bureaucrats.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 284
bare_text: Custody of suitors' deposits and other moneys received by public servants and courts.
plain_english: Any money received by a government officer or a court that isn't government revenue (like bail money or deposits) must be kept in the 'Public Account of India' (or State).
example: If you pay a security deposit to a government court, that money doesn't become government tax money; it sits in a trust account.
extra_content: • The government acts as a banker/trustee for this money.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 285
bare_text: Exemption of property of the Union from State taxation.
plain_english: State governments and local municipalities cannot impose taxes on the property owned by the Central Government.
example: A local municipal corporation cannot demand property tax for a massive Central Railway Station or an Indian Army base located in their city.
extra_content: • Unless Parliament passes a law allowing them to do so.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 286
bare_text: Restrictions as to imposition of tax on the sale or purchase of goods.
plain_english: States CANNOT tax the sale or purchase of goods if the transaction happens outside the state, or during import/export out of India.
example: Maharashtra cannot tax goods that are just sitting in a Mumbai port waiting to be shipped to Dubai.
extra_content: • Protects international trade from arbitrary local taxation.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 287
bare_text: Exemption from taxes on electricity.
plain_english: States cannot impose a tax on electricity that is consumed by the Central Government or used in the construction/operation of railways.
example: The Indian Railways doesn't pay state electricity tax to run its massive electric train network.
extra_content: • Keeps operational costs down for critical federal infrastructure.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 288
bare_text: Exemption from taxation by States in respect of water or electricity in certain cases.
plain_english: States cannot tax the water or electricity generated by a massive inter-state river valley authority (like the Damodar Valley Corporation) without the President's consent.
example: Prevents one state from exploiting a massive multi-state dam project via taxation.
extra_content: • Protects federal infrastructure projects.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 289
bare_text: Exemption of property and income of a State from Union taxation.
plain_english: The Central Government cannot impose taxes on the property and income of a State Government.
example: The Centre cannot demand Income Tax from the profits of a State Road Transport Corporation.
extra_content: • However, if the State runs a commercial trade/business, Parliament CAN tax that specific business income.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 290
bare_text: Adjustment in respect of certain expenses and pensions.
plain_english: Provides a mechanism for adjusting expenses/pensions between the Centre and States if a court or commission serves needs beyond just one government.
example: If a joint commission serves two states, they split the pension costs.
extra_content: • A technical accounting provision.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 291
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: Repealed by the 26th Amendment Act, 1971.
example: This article previously guaranteed 'Privy Purses' (massive tax-free pensions) to the former Kings and Maharajas of princely states.
extra_content: • Indira Gandhi famously abolished the Privy Purses by repealing this article.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 292
bare_text: Borrowing by the Government of India.
plain_english: The Central Government has unlimited power to borrow money on the security of the Consolidated Fund of India, both domestically and internationally.
example: The Centre can issue government bonds or take massive loans from the World Bank.
extra_content: • Parliament has the power to set a legal limit on this borrowing, but it has never done so via a strict absolute cap (FRBM Act acts as a target, not a hard constitutional limit).

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 293
bare_text: Borrowing by States.
plain_english: State governments can borrow money, but ONLY within India. Furthermore, if a state owes any outstanding debt to the Centre, it cannot borrow more money without the Centre's consent.
example: A state like Punjab cannot directly take a loan from a foreign country; it must go through the Centre.
extra_content: • Because almost all states owe money to the Centre, the Centre effectively controls state borrowing limits.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 294
bare_text: Succession to property, assets, rights, liabilities and obligations in certain cases.
plain_english: All property, assets, and debts that belonged to the British Indian government (or the provinces) automatically transferred to the new Union of India (and the new states) upon the Constitution's commencement.
example: The massive railway network built by the British legally became the property of the Republic of India.
extra_content: • Ensured seamless legal succession of the nation's wealth and debts.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 295
bare_text: Succession to property, assets, rights, liabilities and obligations in other cases.
plain_english: Deals with the transfer of assets and liabilities from the former Princely States (Part B states) to the Union and new States.
example: The army and railways owned by the Maharaja of Mysore became the property of the Union government.
extra_content: • Another crucial transitional provision integrating the princely states.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 296
bare_text: Property accruing by escheat or lapse or as bona vacantia.
plain_english: If a person dies without any heirs and without a will, or if property is completely abandoned, it legally becomes the property of the State government (or the Centre, if it involves a Union subject).
example: If a millionaire dies with absolutely no living relatives or will, their mansion 'escheats' to the state government.
extra_content: • Based on the ancient principle that the ultimate owner of all land is the sovereign.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 297
bare_text: Things of value within territorial waters or continental shelf and resources of the exclusive economic zone to vest in the Union.
plain_english: All lands, minerals, and resources found in the ocean within India's territorial waters, continental shelf, and Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) belong exclusively to the Central Government.
example: If massive oil reserves are discovered in the ocean 50 miles off the coast of Gujarat, the oil belongs to the Centre, not Gujarat.
extra_content: • Crucial for national control of offshore oil (like Bombay High) and deep-sea mining.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 298
bare_text: Power to carry on trade, etc.
plain_english: Both the Centre and the States have the executive power to carry on any trade or business, and to acquire/sell property.
example: Allows the government to run massive Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) like Air India, ONGC, or State Transport Corporations.
extra_content: • If the Centre runs a business that is a State subject, it is subject to state laws, and vice versa.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 299
bare_text: Contracts.
plain_english: All government contracts must be made in the name of the President (for the Centre) or the Governor (for the State). However, the President/Governor is NOT personally liable for them.
example: If a contractor builds a highway for the government, the contract says 'President of India', but if the government doesn't pay, the contractor sues the 'Union of India', not the President personally.
extra_content: • Provides sovereign immunity to the heads of state regarding commercial contracts.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 300
bare_text: Suits and proceedings.
plain_english: The Government of India can sue and be sued in the name of the 'Union of India'. State governments can sue and be sued in the name of the State (e.g., 'State of Kerala').
example: If a government truck crashes into your shop, you file a lawsuit against the 'Union of India' or the respective 'State'.
extra_content: • Establishes the legal identity of the government as a juristic person that can be taken to court.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 300A
bare_text: Persons not to be deprived of property save by authority of law.
plain_english: The Right to Property is no longer a Fundamental Right, but it is a Constitutional Right. The government cannot take your land unless there is a specific law authorizing it.
example: If the government wants to build a highway through your farm, they must acquire it legally under the Land Acquisition Act and pay you compensation.
extra_content: • Inserted by the 44th Amendment Act (1978) after deleting Article 31 from Fundamental Rights.

category: Part XIII: Trade, Commerce and Intercourse
title: Article 301
bare_text: Freedom of trade, commerce and intercourse.
plain_english: Trade and commerce shall be completely free throughout the territory of India. States generally cannot block goods coming from other states.
example: Maharashtra cannot ban the import of apples from Himachal Pradesh just to protect its own local farmers.
extra_content: • Ensures India remains a single, unified economic market. Subject to reasonable restrictions in the public interest.

category: Part XIII: Trade, Commerce and Intercourse
title: Article 302
bare_text: Power of Parliament to impose restrictions on trade, commerce and intercourse.
plain_english: Parliament can impose restrictions on the freedom of trade and commerce between states if it is required in the 'public interest'.
example: Parliament could ban the export of a specific essential crop from one state to another to prevent a localized famine.
extra_content: • Acts as a vital exception to the general rule of free trade in Article 301.

category: Part XIII: Trade, Commerce and Intercourse
title: Article 303
bare_text: Restrictions on the legislative powers of the Union and of the States with regard to trade and commerce.
plain_english: Neither Parliament nor State legislatures can make laws that discriminate between one state and another in trade matters, EXCEPT when Parliament needs to deal with a severe scarcity of goods.
example: Parliament cannot pass a law saying 'Factories in Gujarat get a special tax break on shipping that factories in Maharashtra do not get', unless there is a specific scarcity emergency.
extra_content: • Prevents economic favoritism and ensures a level playing field across the nation.

category: Part XIII: Trade, Commerce and Intercourse
title: Article 304
bare_text: Restrictions on trade, commerce and intercourse among States.
plain_english: A state can impose taxes on goods imported from other states ONLY if similar goods produced within that state are also taxed equally. Also, a state can impose reasonable restrictions on trade in the 'public interest', but only with the President's prior approval.
example: Tamil Nadu cannot slap a massive 'import tax' on apples coming from Himachal Pradesh just to make local fruits cheaper; the tax must be equal.
extra_content: • Prevents states from creating internal tariff walls like independent countries.

category: Part XIII: Trade, Commerce and Intercourse
title: Article 305
bare_text: Saving of existing laws and laws providing for State monopolies.
plain_english: Protects existing trade laws and laws that create a total government monopoly over a specific business, completely excluding private citizens.
example: If the government decides that ONLY the state can sell liquor or operate railways, that monopoly cannot be challenged as a violation of free trade.
extra_content: • Empowers the creation of massive Public Sector monopolies.

category: Part XIII: Trade, Commerce and Intercourse
title: Article 306
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: Repealed by the Constitution (Seventh Amendment) Act, 1956.
example: Previously dealt with power of certain Part B States to impose restrictions on trade.
extra_content: • No longer active.

category: Part XIII: Trade, Commerce and Intercourse
title: Article 307
bare_text: Appointment of authority for carrying out the purposes of articles 301 to 304.
plain_english: Parliament can appoint a specific authority (like a commission) to enforce the rules of free trade and commerce across the country.
example: An Inter-State Trade and Commerce Commission could be set up under this article.
extra_content: • Historically, no such permanent authority has been appointed by Parliament.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 308
bare_text: Interpretation.
plain_english: In this Part (Services under the Union and the States), the expression 'State' does not include the State of Jammu and Kashmir.
example: A historical definitional clause.
extra_content: • Effectively modified by the J&K Reorganisation Act, 2019.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 309
bare_text: Recruitment and conditions of service of persons serving the Union or a State.
plain_english: Parliament (or the State Legislature) makes laws to regulate the recruitment and conditions of service (pay, leave, pension) of government employees.
example: The laws that dictate how many days of casual leave an IAS officer gets are made under this article.
extra_content: • Until the legislature makes a law, the President/Governor can make rules for the same.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 310
bare_text: Tenure of office of persons serving the Union or a State.
plain_english: The 'Doctrine of Pleasure': Every person working in the defense or civil services of the Union holds office during the 'pleasure of the President' (or the Governor for state services).
example: Technically, a civil servant works at the pleasure of the head of state and can be dismissed.
extra_content: • However, this absolute 'pleasure' is severely restricted by the massive protections in Article 311.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 311
bare_text: Dismissal, removal or reduction in rank of persons employed in civil capacities under the Union or a State.
plain_english: Provides strong job security to civil servants. They cannot be dismissed by an authority subordinate to the one who appointed them, and they MUST be given a chance to defend themselves in an inquiry.
example: A District Magistrate (IAS officer) appointed by the President cannot be fired by the State Chief Minister.
extra_content: • A core safeguard ensuring civil servants can act independently without fear of arbitrary political firing.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 312
bare_text: All-India services.
plain_english: If the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by a 2/3rds majority, Parliament can create new 'All-India Services' common to both the Centre and the States.
example: The Indian Administrative Service (IAS), Indian Police Service (IPS), and Indian Forest Service (IFoS) are the only three All-India Services.
extra_content: • Officers are recruited by the Centre but serve in the State cadres.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 313
bare_text: Transitional provisions.
plain_english: All laws relating to public services that were in force right before the Constitution began continued to be in force until new laws were made.
example: British-era civil service rules continued to operate smoothly on January 26, 1950.
extra_content: • Ensured the bureaucracy didn't collapse overnight.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 314
bare_text: Repealed.
plain_english: Repealed by the 28th Amendment Act, 1972.
example: Previously guaranteed special protections and conditions of service to old British-era ICS (Indian Civil Service) officers.
extra_content: • Repealed to bring them on par with the modern IAS officers.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 315
bare_text: Public Service Commissions for the Union and for the States.
plain_english: Mandates the creation of the Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) for the Centre, and a State Public Service Commission (SPSC) for each state.
example: The UPSC conducts the incredibly tough Civil Services Examination to recruit IAS and IPS officers.
extra_content: • UPSC members are appointed by the President, SPSC members by the Governor.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 316
bare_text: Appointment and term of office of members.
plain_english: The Chairman and members of the UPSC are appointed by the President. They serve for 6 years or until the age of 65 (whichever is earlier). State PSC members are appointed by the Governor (age limit 62).
example: A highly qualified academic can be appointed to the UPSC for a 6-year term to oversee IAS recruitment.
extra_content: • Nearly half the members must be people who have served the government for at least 10 years.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 317
bare_text: Removal and suspension of a member of a Public Service Commission.
plain_english: A UPSC or SPSC member can only be removed by the President on grounds of 'misbehavior', and ONLY AFTER the Supreme Court holds an inquiry and recommends removal.
example: A Governor cannot fire a State PSC Chairman for political reasons; only the President can, after an SC probe.
extra_content: • Provides massive security of tenure, ensuring the recruitment commission remains completely independent of politicians.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 318
bare_text: Power to make regulations as to conditions of service of members and staff of the Commission.
plain_english: The President (or Governor) makes regulations determining the number of members of the Commission and their conditions of service.
example: The President decides how many total members will sit on the UPSC board.
extra_content: • These conditions cannot be changed to the member's disadvantage after appointment.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 319
bare_text: Prohibition as to the holding of offices by members of Commission on ceasing to be such members.
plain_english: To ensure they aren't bribed with future jobs, a retired UPSC Chairman CANNOT take any further employment under the Government of India or any State.
example: The head of the UPSC cannot be promised a cushy ambassadorship by the Prime Minister in exchange for leaking exam papers.
extra_content: • Other members have slight exceptions (e.g., a UPSC member can become the UPSC Chairman), but general government employment is banned.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 320
bare_text: Functions of Public Service Commissions.
