plain_english: The primary job of the UPSC/SPSC is to conduct examinations for appointments to the services of the Union/State. The government must also consult them on disciplinary matters regarding civil servants.
example: The famous UPSC Civil Services Examination (for IAS, IPS) is mandated by this exact article.
extra_content: • The President/Governor can make rules specifying matters where it is not necessary to consult the Commission.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 321
bare_text: Power to extend functions of Public Service Commissions.
plain_english: Parliament (or the State Legislature) can pass an act to give the Public Service Commission extra duties regarding local authorities, corporations, or public institutions.
example: The state legislature could legally assign the State PSC the task of conducting recruitment exams for the State Electricity Board.
extra_content: • Allows the expansion of the Commission's merit-based recruitment system.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 322
bare_text: Expenses of Public Service Commissions.
plain_english: The expenses of the UPSC (including salaries of its members) are charged directly on the Consolidated Fund of India (or State Fund for SPSC).
example: The Parliament does not vote on the UPSC's budget every year; it is automatically paid.
extra_content: • Another crucial safeguard for their financial independence.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 323
bare_text: Reports of Public Service Commissions.
plain_english: The UPSC must present an annual report of its work to the President, who lays it before Parliament. If the government ignored any advice given by the UPSC, they must attach a memo explaining exactly why they ignored it.
example: If the UPSC recommended firing a corrupt officer, but the government protected him, the government must formally explain themselves to Parliament.
extra_content: • Creates immense pressure on the government to follow the merit-based advice of the Commission.

title: Article 324
bare_text: Superintendence, direction and control of elections to be vested in an Election Commission.
plain_english: Creates the independent Election Commission of India (ECI). It has the absolute power to direct and control all elections to Parliament, State Legislatures, the President, and the Vice-President.
example: During election time, the ECI becomes incredibly powerful, taking control of the police and administration to ensure free and fair voting.
extra_content: • The Chief Election Commissioner can only be removed in the same difficult manner as a Supreme Court judge.

title: Article 325
bare_text: No person to be ineligible for inclusion in, or to claim to be included in a special, electoral roll on grounds of religion, race, caste or sex.
plain_english: There is only ONE general electoral roll (voter list) for every constituency. No one can be excluded from voting, nor can anyone demand a 'special' separate voter list, based on their religion, race, caste, or sex.
example: Abolished the British-era 'Separate Electorates' where only Muslims could vote for Muslim candidates.
extra_content: • The bedrock principle of secular, universal suffrage in India.

title: Article 326
bare_text: Elections to the House of the People and to the Legislative Assemblies of States to be on the basis of adult suffrage.
plain_english: Guarantees Universal Adult Franchise. Every Indian citizen who is at least 18 years old has the fundamental right to vote in national and state elections.
example: No citizen can be denied the right to vote based on religion, race, caste, sex, or lack of wealth.
extra_content: • The voting age was reduced from 21 to 18 by the 61st Amendment Act (1988).

title: Article 327
bare_text: Power of Parliament to make provision with respect to elections to Legislatures.
plain_english: Parliament has the supreme power to make laws regarding all matters relating to elections to both Parliament and State Legislatures, including the preparation of electoral rolls and the delimitation of constituencies.
example: The monumental Representation of the People Act, 1951 (which sets the rules for political parties and disqualifications) was passed under this power.
extra_content: • Centralizes the ultimate rule-making power for elections.

title: Article 328
bare_text: Power of Legislature of a State to make provision with respect to elections to such Legislature.
plain_english: A State Legislature can make laws for its own elections, BUT ONLY for matters that Parliament hasn't already covered.
example: If Parliament missed a specific local detail regarding state elections, the state assembly can fill the gap.
extra_content: • Parliamentary law always overrides State law in electoral matters.

title: Article 329
bare_text: Bar to interference by courts in electoral matters.
plain_english: Courts cannot question the validity of any law relating to the delimitation of constituencies. Furthermore, no election can be challenged EXCEPT by filing an 'Election Petition' after the results are declared.
example: You cannot go to the Supreme Court right in the middle of voting day to halt an election just because you think the voter list is flawed.
extra_content: • Ensures that the massive democratic exercise of elections is not derailed by endless stay orders.

title: Article 330
bare_text: Reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in the House of the People.
plain_english: Mandates that a certain number of seats in the Lok Sabha must be reserved for candidates belonging to the Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs) based on their population ratio.
example: If a constituency is reserved for SCs, only a candidate from the SC community can contest the election there (though everyone votes).
extra_content: • Currently, 84 seats are reserved for SCs and 47 for STs in the Lok Sabha.

title: Article 331
bare_text: Representation of the Anglo-Indian Community in the House of the People.
plain_english: Previously allowed the President to nominate 2 members of the Anglo-Indian community to the Lok Sabha if they were not adequately represented.
example: Historically ensured the tiny Anglo-Indian minority had a voice in Parliament.
extra_content: • **IMPORTANT:** This provision was NOT extended by the 104th Amendment Act in 2019, effectively abolishing this reservation.

title: Article 332
bare_text: Reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in the Legislative Assemblies of the States.
plain_english: Exactly like Article 330, but this mandates reservation for SCs and STs in the State Legislative Assemblies (Vidhan Sabha).
example: Ensures proportional political representation for marginalized communities at the state level.
extra_content: • This reservation is periodically extended by constitutional amendments (currently extended till 2030 by the 104th Amendment).

title: Article 333
bare_text: Representation of the Anglo-Indian community in the Legislative Assemblies of the States.
plain_english: Previously allowed the Governor to nominate 1 member of the Anglo-Indian community to the State Legislative Assembly.
example: States with significant Anglo-Indian populations used this.
extra_content: • **IMPORTANT:** Just like Article 331, this was also discontinued by the 104th Amendment Act in 2019.

title: Article 334
bare_text: Reservation of seats and special representation to cease after certain period.
plain_english: Originally stated that reservations for SCs/STs in legislatures would expire 10 years after the Constitution began. However, this has been continuously extended by constitutional amendments.
example: The 104th Amendment (2019) extended the reservation for SCs and STs for another 10 years (up to 2030), while letting the Anglo-Indian reservation expire.
extra_content: • A constantly debated article requiring periodic renewal.

title: Article 335
bare_text: Claims of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes to services and posts.
plain_english: The claims of SCs and STs must be taken into consideration when making appointments to government jobs, but it must be done consistently with the 'maintenance of efficiency of administration'.
example: This is the core article that balances affirmative action (reservations in jobs) with administrative efficiency.
extra_content: • An amendment later added that the government can lower the qualifying marks for SC/ST candidates in exams without violating the 'efficiency' clause.

title: Article 336
bare_text: Special provision for Anglo-Indian community in certain services.
plain_english: A transitional provision that gave special quotas in railway, customs, and postal services to Anglo-Indians during the first few years after independence.
example: Ensured job security for the community immediately after the British left.
extra_content: • Expired long ago; purely historical now.

title: Article 337
bare_text: Special provision with respect to educational grants for the benefit of Anglo-Indian community.
plain_english: Provided guaranteed educational grants to Anglo-Indian schools for a few years after independence, slowly phasing out.
example: Another transitional protection that has since expired.
extra_content: • No longer active.

title: Article 338
bare_text: National Commission for Scheduled Castes.
plain_english: Creates a constitutional body (NCSC) to investigate and monitor all matters relating to the constitutional safeguards provided for Scheduled Castes.
example: If an SC employee faces systemic discrimination in a government department, the NCSC can summon the department heads like a civil court.
extra_content: • Originally, it was a combined commission for both SCs and STs, but they were split by the 89th Amendment (2003).

title: Article 339
bare_text: Control of the Union over the administration of Scheduled Areas and the welfare of Scheduled Tribes.
plain_english: The President can appoint a Commission to report on the administration of Scheduled Areas. The Centre can also give direct executive orders to a State regarding the execution of schemes essential for the welfare of Scheduled Tribes.
example: If a state is neglecting a vital tribal welfare program, the Centre can constitutionally order them to implement it properly.
extra_content: • Shows the Centre's special overarching responsibility towards tribal populations.

title: Article 340
bare_text: Appointment of a Commission to investigate the conditions of backward classes.
plain_english: Allows the President to appoint a commission to investigate the conditions of socially and educationally backward classes and recommend steps to improve them.
example: The famous Mandal Commission, which led to 27% reservation for OBCs, was appointed under this article.
extra_content: • Forms the historical basis for OBC reservations in India.

title: Article 341
bare_text: Scheduled Castes.
plain_english: The President can, by public notification, specify which castes, races, or tribes are deemed to be 'Scheduled Castes' (SCs) for a specific State or UT.
example: Once the President creates this list, ONLY Parliament can pass a law to add or remove a caste from the SC list. The State government cannot do it.
extra_content: • This creates the official, legal definition of who gets SC reservations.

title: Article 342
bare_text: Scheduled Tribes.
plain_english: The President specifies which tribes or tribal communities are deemed to be 'Scheduled Tribes' (STs) for a specific state.
example: Just like SCs, once the initial Presidential list is made, only Parliament can amend the ST list.
extra_content: • Creates the official, legal definition of who gets ST reservations.

title: Article 343
bare_text: Official language of the Union.
plain_english: Declares that the official language of the Union government is Hindi in the Devanagari script. However, it allowed English to continue being used for official purposes.
example: Government circulars and parliamentary proceedings are usually bilingual (Hindi and English).
extra_content: • India does NOT have a 'National Language', only 'Official Languages'. English continues to be used indefinitely due to the Official Languages Act, 1963.

title: Article 344
bare_text: Commission and Committee of Parliament on official language.
plain_english: The President shall constitute an Official Language Commission (and a Parliamentary Committee) to recommend ways to progressively use Hindi for official purposes, and to restrict the use of English.
example: The B.G. Kher Commission was the first such commission appointed in 1955.
extra_content: • They must consider the 'just claims and interests' of the non-Hindi speaking people regarding government jobs.

title: Article 345
bare_text: Official language or languages of a State.
plain_english: A state legislature can adopt any one or more languages used in the state, or Hindi, as its official language.
example: Tamil Nadu uses Tamil, Karnataka uses Kannada, and Meghalaya uses English as their official state languages.
extra_content: • Allows for linguistic diversity and federalism.

title: Article 346
bare_text: Official language for communication between one State and another or between a State and the Union.
plain_english: The language authorized for use in the Union (currently English and Hindi) is the language for communication between the Centre and States, or between two States.
example: If Kerala writes a letter to the Central Government, it is usually in English. However, if two Hindi-speaking states (like UP and MP) agree, they can communicate entirely in Hindi.
extra_content: • Ensures smooth administrative communication across linguistic borders.

title: Article 347
bare_text: Special provision relating to language spoken by a section of the population of a State.
plain_english: If a substantial proportion of a state's population demands it, the President can order that their spoken language be officially recognized throughout that state or a part of it.
example: If millions of speakers of a minority language in a state feel ignored, the President can force the state to recognize their language for official purposes.
extra_content: • A powerful tool to protect linguistic minorities within states.

title: Article 348
bare_text: Language to be used in the Supreme Court and in the High Courts and for Acts, Bills, etc.
plain_english: All proceedings in the Supreme Court and every High Court MUST be in English. All authoritative texts of bills, acts, and ordinances must also be in English.
example: A lawyer cannot argue a case in the Supreme Court in Hindi without special permission.
extra_content: • Ensures legal uniformity across the country. A Governor can authorize the use of Hindi in a High Court, but the judgments must still be in English.

title: Article 349
bare_text: Special procedure for enactment of certain laws relating to language.
plain_english: During the first 15 years of the Constitution, no bill regarding the language used in the Supreme/High Courts or for Acts/Bills could be introduced without the President's prior sanction.
example: A transitional provision to prevent sudden, disruptive linguistic changes in the legal system.
extra_content: • Historically relevant, less practical impact today.

title: Article 350
bare_text: Language to be used in representations for redress of grievances.
plain_english: Every person has the fundamental right to submit a petition or grievance to ANY government officer in ANY language used in the Union or the State.
example: A villager in Maharashtra can submit a written complaint to a government officer in Marathi, and the officer cannot reject it by demanding it be written in English.
extra_content: • Ensures language is never a barrier for a citizen seeking help from the state.

title: Article 351
bare_text: Directive for development of the Hindi language.
plain_english: It is the duty of the Union Government to promote the spread of the Hindi language, and to develop it so that it may serve as a medium of expression for all the elements of the composite culture of India.
example: The government funds the Central Hindi Directorate to create standard vocabularies and promote the language.
extra_content: • Crucially, it directs that Hindi should be enriched by drawing primarily on Sanskrit for its vocabulary.

title: Article 352: National Emergency
bare_text: Proclamation of Emergency.
plain_english: The President can declare a National Emergency if India is threatened by war, external aggression, or 'armed rebellion'.
example: A National Emergency was famously declared in 1975 by Indira Gandhi (using the old phrase 'internal disturbance', which was later removed).
extra_content:
• Can only be declared on the WRITTEN advice of the Cabinet.
  • Changes the federal structure into a unitary one.

title: Article 353
bare_text: Effect of Proclamation of Emergency.
plain_english: When a National Emergency (Art 352) is active, the executive power of the Centre expands to give direct orders to any State regarding ANY matter. Furthermore, Parliament can make laws on ANY subject (even State List subjects).
example: The Centre can effectively take over the entire administration of the country, transforming India from a federal system into a unitary one.
extra_content: • Even states where the emergency is not actively occurring can be controlled if the security situation demands it.

title: Article 354
bare_text: Application of provisions relating to distribution of revenues while a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation.
plain_english: During a National Emergency, the President can modify the constitutional rules regarding how tax money is shared between the Centre and the States.
example: The Centre can legally keep a larger share of the tax revenue to fund a war effort, drastically reducing the money sent to the states.
extra_content: • This order must be laid before Parliament and expires when the Emergency ends.

title: Article 355
bare_text: Duty of the Union to protect States against external aggression and internal disturbance.
plain_english: It is the absolute constitutional duty of the Central Government to protect every State against external war and internal rebellion, AND to ensure the state government is carried out in accordance with the Constitution.
example: If massive riots break out in a state and the local police fail, the Centre has a duty to send in the CRPF or Army to restore order, even if the state CM doesn't ask for it.
extra_content: • This article provides the philosophical justification for imposing President's Rule (Art 356) if a state's constitutional machinery completely fails.

title: Article 356: President's Rule (State Emergency)
bare_text: Provisions in case of failure of constitutional machinery in States.
plain_english: Known as 'President's Rule'. If a state government cannot be carried on in accordance with the Constitution, the Centre can dismiss the state government and take direct control.
example: If a Chief Minister loses their majority in the assembly and no other party can form a government, President's Rule is imposed.
extra_content: • Historically, the most misused article in the Constitution, though the S.R. Bommai Supreme Court judgment drastically curtailed its misuse.

title: Article 357
bare_text: Exercise of legislative powers under Proclamation issued under article 356.
plain_english: When President's Rule (Art 356) is imposed in a state, Parliament takes over the state's lawmaking power. Parliament can then delegate this lawmaking power to the President to make 'President's Acts' for the state.
example: Because Parliament is too busy to pass everyday state laws for a state under President's Rule, the President (advised by the Union Cabinet) just passes executive laws for that state.
extra_content: • Laws made under this provision continue to be active even after President's Rule ends, until the new state legislature repeals or alters them.

title: Article 358
bare_text: Suspension of provisions of article 19 during emergencies.
plain_english: When a National Emergency is declared (due to war or external aggression), all the six fundamental freedoms under Article 19 (speech, assembly, movement, etc.) are AUTOMATICALLY suspended.
example: During a war with another country, the government can ban protests or restrict press freedom without passing new laws.
extra_content: • Article 19 is NOT suspended if the emergency is declared due to 'armed rebellion' (internal).

title: Article 359
bare_text: Suspension of the enforcement of the rights conferred by Part III during emergencies.
plain_english: The President can issue an order to suspend the right to move any court to enforce other Fundamental Rights during a National Emergency.
example: If suspended, you cannot file a writ in the Supreme Court claiming your rights were violated.
extra_content: • **Crucial exception:** The rights under Article 20 (protection against arbitrary conviction) and Article 21 (right to life and liberty) CAN NEVER BE SUSPENDED.

title: Article 360
bare_text: Provisions as to financial emergency.
plain_english: If the financial stability or credit of India is threatened, the President can declare a Financial Emergency, allowing the Centre to drastically reduce salaries of government employees and judges.
example: During a severe economic collapse, the Centre could order states to slash budgets and cut minister salaries.
extra_content: • A Financial Emergency has NEVER been declared in the history of India (not even during the 1991 economic crisis).

title: Article 361
bare_text: Protection of President and Governors.
plain_english: The President and Governors have absolute legal immunity. They cannot be sued or arrested for any official act, and no criminal proceedings can be started against them while they are in office.
example: Even if the President commits a crime, the police cannot arrest them as long as they are sitting in the Rashtrapati Bhavan. They must be impeached first.
extra_content: • Civil proceedings for personal acts can only be started after giving 2 months' notice.

title: Article 362
bare_text: Rights and privileges of Rulers of Indian States.
plain_english: Repealed by the 26th Amendment Act, 1971.
example: Previously protected the personal rights, privileges, and dignities of the former Maharajas.
extra_content: • Repealed along with the Privy Purses.

title: Article 363
bare_text: Bar to interference by courts in disputes arising out of certain treaties, agreements, etc.
plain_english: The Supreme Court and other courts have ZERO jurisdiction to intervene in any disputes arising out of the original treaties and agreements signed between the Government of India and the Princely States before the Constitution.
example: If a former royal family claims the government broke the 1948 treaty regarding their palace, they cannot sue the government in court.
extra_content: • Ensured that the massive political integration of India couldn't be derailed by endless lawsuits from former kings.

title: Article 364
bare_text: Special provisions as to major ports and aerodromes.
plain_english: The President can declare that any specific law made by Parliament or a State will NOT apply to a 'major port' or 'aerodrome' (airport), or will only apply with massive modifications.
example: Ensures that critical national infrastructure like the Mumbai Port or Delhi Airport can be governed by special rules if needed, avoiding local legal tangles.
extra_content: • Protects strategic transportation hubs.

title: Article 365
bare_text: Effect of failure to comply with, or to give effect to, directions given by the Union.
plain_english: If a State government refuses to follow a lawful executive direction given by the Central Government, the President can legally conclude that the state's constitutional machinery has failed.
example: If the Centre orders a state to protect a federal railway line and the state flat out refuses, the Centre can use this article as the trigger to impose President's Rule (Article 356) and sack the state government.
extra_content: • The ultimate enforcement mechanism for federal supremacy.

title: Article 366
bare_text: Definitions.
plain_english: The massive dictionary of the Constitution. It legally defines exactly what words like 'Agricultural income', 'Corporation tax', 'Existing law', 'Goods', and 'State' mean throughout the entire document.
example: If there's a dispute over whether electricity is considered 'goods' for taxation, lawyers look at the definition in this article.
extra_content: • Vital for interpreting the complex legal language of the document.

title: Article 367
bare_text: Interpretation.
plain_english: Unless the context otherwise requires, the rules of the General Clauses Act, 1897 apply for interpreting this Constitution, just as they apply for interpreting an ordinary Act of Parliament.
example: A highly technical legal rule that tells judges exactly which rulebook to use when interpreting ambiguous grammar or phrases.
extra_content: • Ensures consistency in legal interpretation.

category: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
title: Article 368: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution
bare_text: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor.
plain_english: Gives Parliament the power to amend (change, add, or delete) parts of the Constitution. Most amendments require a 'Special Majority' (2/3rds of members present and voting).
example: Changing the voting age from 21 to 18 required an amendment under Article 368.
extra_content:
• However, Parliament CANNOT amend the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution (as ruled in the famous Kesavananda Bharati case).
  • Amendments affecting the federal structure also require ratification by half the states.

title: Article 369
bare_text: Temporary power to Parliament to make laws with respect to certain matters in the State List as if they were matters in the Concurrent List.
plain_english: Gave Parliament temporary power (for 5 years after 1950) to make laws on specific State List subjects (like trade in cotton, food-stuffs, and iron) to handle post-war economic shortages.
example: A purely transitional provision to handle the chaotic economy immediately after independence and WWII.
extra_content: • Expired in 1955.

title: Article 370
bare_text: Temporary provisions with respect to the State of Jammu and Kashmir.
plain_english: Granted a special autonomous status to Jammu and Kashmir, allowing it to have its own constitution and limiting the Centre's legislative powers over it.
example: For decades, many Indian laws did not automatically apply to J&K.
extra_content: • The special status was effectively abrogated by a Presidential Order in August 2019. The article still exists in the text but is virtually inoperative.

title: Article 371
bare_text: Special provision with respect to the States of Maharashtra and Gujarat.
plain_english: The first of many 'Special Provisions' (ranging from 371A to 371J) designed to protect the unique cultural, economic, or tribal interests of specific states.
example: Article 371A grants special protections to Nagaland regarding Naga customary law, while 371J provides special status to the Hyderabad-Karnataka region.
extra_content: • Unlike 370 which was 'temporary', the 371 series provisions are generally considered 'special' and permanent accommodations for regional diversity.

title: Article 371A
bare_text: Special provision with respect to the State of Nagaland.
plain_english: Massive special autonomy for Nagaland. No Act of Parliament regarding Naga religion, customary law, or ownership/transfer of land applies to Nagaland UNLESS the Nagaland Legislative Assembly specifically votes to adopt it.
example: Central laws regarding property inheritance do not apply in Nagaland; they follow their own Naga customary laws.
extra_content: • The Governor also has special responsibilities for law and order due to historical insurgencies.

title: Article 371J
bare_text: Special provision with respect to the State of Karnataka.
plain_english: Provides special status to the six backward districts of the Hyderabad-Karnataka region. Allows for a separate development board and reservations in local jobs and education.
example: Ensures that a specific percentage of government jobs in this backward region are reserved strictly for people born in that region.
extra_content: • Added very recently (2012) to address massive regional imbalances.

title: Article 372
bare_text: Continuance in force of existing laws and their adaptation.
plain_english: Crucial transitional article: All the laws that existed in India before 1950 (the British-era laws) continue to remain in force until they are altered or repealed by the new Parliament.
example: This is why the Indian Penal Code (IPC) from 1860 and the Indian Contract Act (1872) remained the law of the land even after India became a Republic.
extra_content: • Allowed the President to make minor adaptations to these old laws to make them fit the new Constitution.

title: Article 373
bare_text: Power of President to make order in respect of persons under preventive detention in certain cases.
plain_english: Gave the President temporary power (for the first year) to make rules regarding Preventive Detention until Parliament could pass a formal law.
example: Ensured the government had the power to detain suspected threats immediately after independence.
extra_content: • Expired long ago; superseded by Acts like the National Security Act.

title: Article 374
bare_text: Provisions as to Judges of the Federal Court and proceedings pending in the Federal Court or before His Majesty in Council.
plain_english: The judges of the old British-era 'Federal Court' automatically became the judges of the new 'Supreme Court' on Jan 26, 1950. All pending cases were transferred.
example: Ensured the judiciary didn't stop working for even a single day during the transition to a Republic.
extra_content: • Also officially cut off the old practice of appealing Indian cases to the Privy Council in London.

title: Article 375
bare_text: Courts, authorities and officers to continue to function subject to the provisions of the Constitution.
plain_english: All courts (like High Courts) and all authorities/officers across India simply continued doing their jobs on January 26, 1950, just under the new Constitution.
example: A police inspector who went to sleep under the King's rule woke up as an inspector under the Republic without losing his job.
extra_content: • The ultimate 'seamless transition' clause.

title: Article 376
bare_text: Provisions as to Judges of High Courts.
plain_english: Similar to 374, all existing judges of provincial High Courts automatically became judges of the new state High Courts.
example: Guaranteed job continuity for the higher judiciary.
extra_content: • Transitional provision.

title: Article 377
bare_text: Provisions as to Comptroller and Auditor-General of India.
plain_english: The existing Auditor-General of India automatically became the new Comptroller and Auditor-General (CAG) of the Republic.
example: Ensured the nation's accounts were continuously monitored.
extra_content: • Transitional provision.

title: Article 378
bare_text: Provisions as to Public Service Commissions.
plain_english: The members of the existing public service commissions simply continued as members of the new UPSC and State PSCs.
example: Ensured recruitment of bureaucrats wasn't interrupted.
extra_content: • Transitional provision.

title: Article 379
bare_text: Provisions as to provisional Parliament and the Speaker and Deputy Speaker thereof.
plain_english: Repealed. Originally stated that the Constituent Assembly itself would act as the 'Provisional Parliament' until the first general elections could be held in 1951-52.
example: This is how India had a working Parliament before the first elections took place.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956 as it had served its purpose.

title: Article 380
bare_text: Provision as to President.
plain_english: Repealed. Originally allowed the Constituent Assembly to elect a 'Provisional President' (Dr. Rajendra Prasad) until a formal electoral college could be formed.
example: Allowed India to have a Head of State on Day 1 of the Republic.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 381
bare_text: Council of Ministers of the President.
plain_english: Repealed. Originally ensured that the existing Ministers (Jawaharlal Nehru's cabinet) continued to advise the Provisional President.
example: Provided executive continuity.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 382
bare_text: Provisions as to provisional Legislatures for States in Part A of the First Schedule.
plain_english: Repealed. Allowed existing provincial assemblies to act as provisional state legislatures.
example: State-level equivalent of Article 379.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 383
bare_text: Provision as to Governors of Provinces.
plain_english: Repealed. Allowed existing Governors to continue in their posts.
example: Provided state executive continuity.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 384
bare_text: Council of Ministers of Governors.
plain_english: Repealed. Allowed existing state ministers to continue.
example: State-level equivalent of Article 381.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 385
bare_text: Provision as to provisional Legislatures in States in Part B of the First Schedule.
plain_english: Repealed. Dealt with the provisional legislatures for the former princely states.
example: Transitional provision.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 386
bare_text: Council of Ministers for States in Part B of the First Schedule.
plain_english: Repealed. Dealt with provisional ministers for former princely states.
example: Transitional provision.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 387
bare_text: Special provision as to determination of population for the purposes of certain elections.
plain_english: Repealed. Allowed the President to estimate populations for the very first elections before a proper census could be fully utilized.
example: A pragmatic fix to get the first elections running.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 388
bare_text: Provisions as to the filling of casual vacancies in the provisional Parliament and provisional Legislatures of the States.
plain_english: Repealed. Set the rules for how to fill a seat if a member of the provisional parliament died before the first elections.
example: Transitional provision.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 389
bare_text: Provision as to Bills pending in the Dominion Legislature and in the Legislatures of Provinces and Indian States.
plain_english: Repealed. Allowed bills that were half-passed before independence to continue being debated without starting over.
example: Saved time on pending legislation.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 390
bare_text: Moneys received or raised or expenditure incurred between the commencement of the Constitution and the 31st day of March, 1950.
plain_english: Repealed. A highly specific accounting rule to handle government money during the 2-month gap between Republic Day (Jan 26) and the end of the financial year (March 31).
example: Transitional financial provision.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 391
bare_text: Power of the President to amend the First and Fourth Schedules in certain contingencies.
plain_english: Repealed. Gave the President temporary power to modify state names and boundaries right at the beginning.
example: Transitional provision.
extra_content: • Repealed in 1956.

title: Article 392
bare_text: Power of the President to remove difficulties.
plain_english: The President had the power to make special orders to 'remove any difficulties' in transitioning to the new Constitution.
example: A 'Henry VIII clause' giving the executive massive power to fix constitutional bugs on the fly during the crucial first few years.
extra_content: • This power expired after the first Parliament met. (A similar clause was controversially used regarding Article 370 in 2019).

title: Article 393
bare_text: Short title.
plain_english: Simply states the official legal name of this massive document.
example: This Constitution may be called the 'Constitution of India'.
extra_content: • The shortest article in the Constitution.

title: Article 394
bare_text: Commencement.
plain_english: States that while the main Constitution came into force on January 26, 1950 (Republic Day), a few specific articles (like Citizenship and Elections) came into force instantly on November 26, 1949 (when it was adopted).
example: This allowed the Election Commission to be set up immediately before the Republic was even formally declared.
extra_content: • A crucial article detailing the exact timeline of India's birth as a Republic.

title: Article 395
bare_text: Repeals.
plain_english: This is the very last article of the Constitution. It officially repealed (cancelled) the old British laws that governed India, specifically the Government of India Act 1935 and the Indian Independence Act 1947.
example: The moment the Constitution came into force on Jan 26, 1950, this article ensured India cut its final legal umbilical cord with the British Parliament.
extra_content: • The ultimate symbol of total legal sovereignty.
